verses.commentary: Jesus speaks in parables. A man plants a vineyard, puts a wall, digs a winepress, builds a watchtower. Isaiah 5:1–7. Vineyard = Israel. He rents it to tenants, goes away. God entrusts Israel to leaders.
At harvest he sends a servant to collect fruit. The prophets sent to Israel.
They seize, beat, send him away empty. Israel's treatment of the prophets.
He sends another servant. They strike his head, treat him shamefully. Repeated rejection.
He sends another, they kill him. Many others: some beaten, others killed. Long history of prophetic martyrdom. 2 Chronicles 36:15–16.
He had one left to send, a son, whom he loved. He sent him last, saying: They will respect my son. Final emissary. The beloved son. Jesus identifies himself.
The tenants say: This is the heir. Let's kill him, and the inheritance will be ours. Malice and greed. They recognize the son but choose murder.
They took him, killed him, threw him out of the vineyard. Prophetic of Jesus' death outside Jerusalem (Hebrews 13:12).
What will the owner do? He will come, kill those tenants, give the vineyard to others. Judgment on Israel's leaders. The kingdom transferred. The church receives what Israel's leaders rejected.
Haven't you read: The stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone? Psalm 118:22. Jesus is the rejected stone, now exalted.
The Lord has done this, and it is marvelous. God's sovereign reversal. Rejection becomes exaltation.
Chief priests, teachers, elders want to arrest him. They know he spoke against them. But they fear the crowd. Truth hits its target.
They send Pharisees and Herodians to trap him. Unlikely alliance. Pharisees oppose Rome; Herodians support it. United only in opposing Jesus.

Teacher, you are a man of integrity, not swayed by others, you teach God's way in truth. Flattery precedes the trap. Is it right to pay taxes to Caesar or not? Dilemma: say yes, offend the people (who resent Roman occupation); say no, commit treason.
Jesus knows their hypocrisy. Why test me? Bring me a denarius. He exposes their intent, asks for evidence.
Whose image and inscription? Caesar's. They have the coin. They participate in the Roman economy.
Give back to Caesar what is Caesar's and to God what is God's. Brilliant answer. Dual obligation: civic and sacred. Render to each what belongs. They are amazed. The trap fails.
Sadducees (who deny resurrection) come with a question. Different party, same goal: discredit Jesus.
Moses said if a man's brother dies childless, he must marry the widow and raise offspring. Levirate marriage (Deuteronomy 25:5–6).
Seven brothers. The first married and died childless.
The second married her, also died childless. Same with the third.
All seven married her, left no children. Last, the woman died.
At the resurrection, whose wife will she be? Ridiculous scenario designed to mock resurrection doctrine.
Jesus: You are in error because you do not know the Scriptures or the power of God. Double ignorance. They misunderstand revelation and divine capability.

When the dead rise, they will neither marry nor be given in marriage. They will be like angels in heaven. Resurrection life transcends earthly categories. Marriage is for this age, not the age to come.
About the dead rising, have you not read in Moses, at the burning bush: I am the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob? Exodus 3:6. Present tense: I am, not I was.
The patriarchs live. Resurrection is certain.
A teacher of the law, hearing the debate, notices Jesus gave a good answer. He asks: Which commandment is most important? Sincere question, not a trap.
Jesus: The most important is: Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is one. Deuteronomy 6:4. The Shema. Monotheism is foundational.
Love the Lord your God with all your heart, soul, mind, and strength. Deuteronomy 6:5. Total devotion. All faculties engaged.
The second is: Love your neighbor as yourself. Leviticus 19:18. No commandment greater than these. Jesus unites the two. Love of God and love of neighbor are inseparable.
The scribe agrees: Well said, teacher. You are right. God is one, no other but him. Affirms Jesus' answer.
To love God and love neighbor is more important than all burnt offerings and sacrifices. Remarkable insight from a scribe. Echoes 1 Samuel 15:22, Hosea 6:6, Micah 6:6–8. Obedience exceeds ritual.
Jesus: You are not far from the kingdom of God. High praise. Close, but not yet in. Understanding is not yet faith. From then on no one dared ask him questions. Jesus silences all challengers.
Jesus teaching in temple courts asks: Why do teachers say the Messiah is David's son? Jesus now asks the questions.
David himself, speaking by the Holy Spirit, said: The Lord said to my Lord, Sit at my right hand until I put your enemies under your feet. Psalm 110:1. David calls the Messiah Lord.

David calls him Lord. How then can he be his son? If Messiah is David's descendant, why does David call him Lord? Answer: Messiah is both human (David's son) and divine (David's Lord). Jesus claims transcendence.
Watch out for the teachers of the law. They like flowing robes, respectful greetings in marketplaces. Love of status and recognition.
Most important seats in synagogues, places of honor at banquets. Hierarchical positioning. Pride displayed.
They devour widows' houses and for a show make lengthy prayers. These will be punished most severely. Exploitation masked by piety. Judgment awaits.
Jesus sits opposite the temple treasury, watches people give. Many rich throw in large amounts. Public display.
A poor widow puts in two small copper coins, worth a few cents. Tiny offering.
Jesus: Truly, this widow has put in more than all the others. Paradox. How can the smallest be the largest?

They gave out of their wealth; she out of her poverty put in everything, all she had to live on. Proportionality. They gave surplus; she gave substance. Total sacrifice. God measures generosity not by amount but by cost. She models discipleship: give everything, trust God. Chapter twelve: parables, taxes, resurrection, commandments, David's son, widow's offering. Jesus confronts, silences, teaches. The religious establishment is condemned; the poor widow is commended. The kingdom inverts all values.
